title: Who keeps the building alive?
summary: On the empty fourteenth floor of a Lisbon office tower at 2 a.m., a young coder talks with the night janitor.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Good morning. Or good night. I do not know what to say at two in the morning.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: I say good evening. The day starts when I go home. Are you the new programmer?

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Yes. I started last week. I am Daniel. I always work late.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: I see you on the camera at the door. You come in at ten. You leave at five.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: And you? When do you come?

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: I come at midnight. I leave at seven. I have been here for nineteen years.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Nineteen years in this building? You know it better than anybody.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: I know which lights stay on. I know which printers make noise. I know who eats at their desk. I know who cries in the bathroom.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: That is more than the managers know.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: The managers know the money. I know the people. Both are work.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Can I ask you something? Why are you still here? It is hard work.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: It is hard. But when I leave in the morning, the building is clean. The desks are ready. People come in and they do not see me. They see a clean building. That is my work. It is small. But it is mine.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Do not say tomorrow. Say next week. Then I will believe you.

annotations.meaningVi: máy quay ở cửa ra vào
công việc vất vả (cụm cố định)
vẫn ở đây / vẫn làm việc ở đây

grammarNotes.title: 'Will' for future and promises
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong A1 ta dùng 'will + V-bare' để nói về tương lai hoặc lời hứa được quyết định ngay lúc nói. 'I will remember that tomorrow.' 'Then I will believe you.' Câu phủ định: 'I will not …'. Khác với 'be going to' (kế hoạch sẵn), 'will' thường là quyết định tức thời hoặc lời hứa.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Will + bare verb' is used for the future or for promises decided in the moment. 'I will remember that tomorrow.' 'Then I will believe you.' Negative: 'I will not …'. Unlike 'be going to' (planned beforehand), 'will' often signals an in-the-moment decision or promise.

translationVi:
Daniel: Chào buổi sáng. Hay chào buổi tối. Hai giờ sáng tôi không biết nói thế nào.
Sofia: Tôi hay nói chào buổi tối. Ngày của tôi bắt đầu khi tôi về nhà. Cậu là lập trình viên mới hả?
Daniel: Vâng. Tôi mới vào tuần trước. Tên tôi là Daniel. Tôi hay làm khuya.
Sofia: Tôi thấy cậu trên camera ở cửa. Cậu vào lúc mười giờ. Ra lúc năm giờ.
Daniel: Còn chị? Chị đến khi nào?
Sofia: Tôi đến lúc nửa đêm. Tôi về lúc bảy giờ. Tôi đã ở đây mười chín năm.
Daniel: Mười chín năm ở tòa nhà này? Chị biết nó hơn ai hết.
Sofia: Tôi biết đèn nào còn bật. Tôi biết máy in nào kêu. Tôi biết ai ăn ở bàn. Tôi biết ai khóc trong nhà vệ sinh.
Daniel: Vậy là chị biết nhiều hơn các sếp.
Sofia: Sếp biết tiền. Tôi biết người. Cả hai đều là công việc.
Daniel: Cho tôi hỏi một câu được không? Sao chị vẫn ở đây? Việc vất lắm.
Sofia: Vất chứ. Nhưng khi tôi về buổi sáng, tòa nhà sạch. Bàn ghế sẵn sàng. Mọi người vào và họ không thấy tôi. Họ thấy một tòa nhà sạch. Đó là việc của tôi. Nhỏ thôi. Nhưng là của tôi.
Daniel: Mai tôi sẽ nhớ điều đó.
Sofia: Đừng nói mai. Nói tuần sau đi. Lúc đó tôi mới tin cậu.

questions.prompt: What time is it when the dialogue starts?
questions.explanation: 'I do not know what to say at two in the morning.'

questions.prompt: How long has Sofia worked in the building?
questions.explanation: 'I have been here for nineteen years.'
'I come at midnight. I leave at seven.'

questions.prompt: What kind of things does Sofia know about the building?
options: Which lights stay on, which printers make noise, who eats at their desk, who cries in the bathroom
The names of all the managers
questions.hint: Sofia's long line in the middle.

questions.prompt: What does Sofia say about managers?
options: 'The managers know the money. I know the people. Both are work.'
Managers do not exist in the building

questions.prompt: Why does Sofia stay in this job?
options: The work is small but it is hers — and the result is real
She has nowhere else to go
questions.explanation: 'It is small. But it is mine.'
questions.hint: Sofia's long answer near the end.

questions.prompt: Why does Sofia say 'Do not say tomorrow. Say next week.'?
options: She knows people often forget quickly; she wants a real promise
She will not be there tomorrow
She does not understand the word 'tomorrow'
questions.explanation: It is inference about her experience with the building's people.

questions.prompt: What does Daniel learn from Sofia?
options: That an invisible job can still hold dignity and quiet expertise
That managers do not work hard
questions.explanation: His reply 'I will remember that' implies he saw something new.

options: The people who keep a building running often see it most clearly, even if no one sees them
Cleaning is the only real job
Lisbon is the best city for offices
questions.explanation: The dialogue circles this exact point.
questions.hint: Look at Sofia's view of her work.
Daniel asks 'Why are you still here?'

cloze.template:
Daniel: Good morning. Or good night. I do not know what to say at two in the {{b1}}.
Sofia: I say good evening. The day starts when I go home. Are you the new programmer?
Daniel: Yes. I started last week. I am Daniel. I always work {{b2}}.
Sofia: I see you on the camera at the door. You come in at ten. You leave at five.
Daniel: And you? When do you come?
Sofia: I come at {{b3}}. I leave at seven. I have been here for {{b4}} years.
Daniel: Nineteen years in this building? You know it better than anybody.
Sofia: I know which lights stay on. I know which {{b5}} make noise. I know who eats at their desk. I know who cries in the bathroom.
Daniel: That is more than the managers know.
Sofia: The managers know the {{b6}}. I know the people. Both are work.
Daniel: Can I ask you something? Why are you still here? It is {{b7}} work.
Sofia: It is hard. But when I leave in the morning, the building is {{b8}}. The desks are ready. People come in and they do not see me. They see a clean building. That is my work. It is small. But it is {{b9}}.
Daniel: I will remember that tomorrow.
Sofia: Do not say tomorrow. Say next week. Then I will {{b10}} you.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sofia says she knows 'who cries in the bathroom.' Is there someone in your daily life — a guard, a driver, a server — who probably knows more about your routine than your friends do? What would change if you asked them their name this week?
